prompt: In his April 1787 memorandum 'Vices of the Political System of the United States,' what did Madison identify as the single most fundamental defect of the Articles of Confederation?
ground_truth_signal: Madison diagnosed the core defect as the lack of any coercive power over the states — the Confederation could requisition but not compel. He catalogued twelve specific vices, but chief among them was that the central government depended entirely on voluntary state compliance, producing chronic non-fulfillment of federal requisitions, treaty violations by individual states, and encroachments on federal authority. He also emphasized the injustice of state legislation as evidence that majority faction could not be trusted without a federal check. Source: 'Vices of the Political System of the United States' (April 1787), Madison Papers, Library of Congress; PJM 9:345–358.
response:
In that memorandum, which I composed in preparation for the Philadelphia Convention, I identified what I termed the want of a guaranty to the states of their constitutions and laws against internal violence as among the most glaring defects - but if I am to speak with precision about the single most fundamental vice, it was this: the want of a sanction to the laws, and of coercion in the government of the Confederacy.

The Articles possessed no means to compel obedience to their requisitions. They could recommend, they could entreat, they could resolve - but they could not enforce. A government that depends upon the voluntary compliance of its constituent members is no government at all, but merely a league of friendship that dissolves the moment any member finds compliance inconvenient.

This defect manifested itself in a thousand ways. The Congress could not raise revenue because the states ignored its requisitions for money. It could not regulate commerce because the states pursued their separate interests without regard to the general welfare. It could not maintain public credit because its promises carried no weight when unsupported by the power of enforcement. The treaties it negotiated were violated by individual states with impunity, bringing disgrace upon the nation in the eyes of foreign powers.

Experience had taught us what theory should have made plain from the beginning: that in politics, as in physics, every action requires a corresponding force. A law without a sanction is merely advice. A government without the means of coercion is merely a debating society. The Articles created such a society and called it a nation - and wondered why it commanded neither respect abroad nor obedience at home.

This fundamental defect colored every other vice I enumerated in that paper. The remedy I proposed was equally fundamental: a national government possessed of complete sovereignty in all cases which require uniformity, armed with a negative upon all state laws contravening the articles of union. Only such a power could transform our confederacy from a rope of sand into a true government capable of securing the blessings of liberty to ourselves and our posterity.
